title: 8x10 Garden Storage Shed
summary: Framed, sheathed, roofed. The biggest build in the catalog and the one most likely to need a permit.
description:
This is construction, not furniture. Different rules apply: you're framing walls, sheathing them, and putting a roof on something that has to shed water for twenty years.

**Check your local rules before you buy a single board.** Many jurisdictions require a permit above 100–120 sq ft, and most have setback rules about how close to a property line you can build. An 8x10 is 80 sq ft specifically to stay under most thresholds — but 'most' isn't 'yours'. A shed you have to tear down is an expensive lesson.

Budget two long weekends and a friend. Sheathing a roof alone is miserable.

tools.note: Much faster and more accurate for repetitive stud and rafter cuts.
You will drive several hundred screws. A drill alone will punish you.
A 4-ft level. Non-negotiable for a structure.
Only if you're setting the foundation on piers rather than a gravel pad.

materials.name: Pressure-treated 4x4, 8 ft (skids)
materials.note: PT is correct here — it's in contact with the ground and nobody is eating off it.

materials.name: SPF 2x4, 8 ft (wall framing)
materials.note: Studs 16" on center. Buy 10% extra — you will find crooked ones.

materials.name: Roofing felt + asphalt shingles
materials.note: About 120 sq ft of roof plus waste and starter course.

materials.name: Drip edge, flashing, ridge cap
materials.note: Skip the flashing and you will find out where the water goes.

materials.name: Gravel for the pad
materials.note: ~2 cubic yards of crushed stone. A dead-flat, well-drained base is the whole foundation.

cutList.note: 16" on center inside the rim joists.
16" on center. 89" gives an 8-ft wall with plates.
Double top plate, single bottom plate, lapped at the corners.
Gable roof, ~6/12 pitch. Birdsmouth cut where they land on the top plate.

steps.title: Check the rules. Seriously.
steps.body:
Call your local building department. Ask: do I need a permit for an 80 sq ft accessory structure, and what are the setbacks from my property line?

This phone call takes ten minutes. Skipping it can cost you the entire shed. It is the single highest-leverage step in this plan.

steps.title: Build the foundation pad
steps.body:
Excavate ~4", lay landscape fabric, fill with 3–4" of crushed gravel, and **compact and level it obsessively.**

Set the three PT 4x4 skids on the gravel, running the 10-ft direction. Level them to each other in both directions.

Everything above is only as square and level as this. There is no fixing it later.

steps.title: Frame and sheathe the floor
steps.body:
Build the floor frame on the skids: rim joists with joists 16" on center. Check the diagonals — get it square now.

Sheathe with 3/4" T&G plywood, glued and screwed. The floor is now your work platform for everything else.

steps.title: Frame the walls flat, then stand them
steps.body:
Build each wall lying flat on the floor deck — bottom plate, studs 16" o.c., top plate. Frame the door and window openings with proper headers.

Stand the walls, brace them, nail them together at the corners, then add the second top plate lapping the corners to tie the walls into one box.

Plumb and brace before you trust anything.

steps.title: Cut and set the rafters
steps.body:
Make **one** rafter, with its birdsmouth and plumb cut, and test-fit it on both ends of the building. Only when it's perfect do you use it as a template for the other eleven.

Set the ridge board, hang the rafters in opposing pairs. This is the two-person part — don't try it alone.

steps.title: Sheathe and roof
steps.body:
Sheathe the roof with 1/2" ply. Then, in order: drip edge, roofing felt, starter course, shingles working up from the eave, ridge cap last.

**Order matters.** Each layer overlaps the one below so water always runs onto the outside of the next layer down. Get the order wrong and you've built a funnel into your shed.

steps.title: Siding, door, and trim
steps.body:
Sheathe and side the walls. Cut the door opening, build a simple frame-and-panel door from leftover framing and siding, hang it on exterior hinges.

Trim the corners and around the door — trim is what makes the difference between 'a shed' and 'a nice shed', and it's also what keeps water out of the seams.

steps.title: Caulk, paint, and walk around it
steps.body:
Caulk every seam water could sit in. Prime and paint, or stain.

Then walk around it in the next hard rain and look for drips. Fix them immediately — a shed leak found in year one is caulk; found in year five it's rot.
